Client 1: I think it’s around one-forty a year, give or take.
Client 2: Isn’t it closer to one-thirty?
Client 1: No—because of the pension. With that, it’s about $139,950 gross for the year.
Advisor: Got it—about $139,950 gross household. Thanks for correcting that. Now, William, can you break down what makes up your income streams? Even just the buckets.
Client 1: Sure. There’s a pension monthly, Social Security monthly, a little business income monthly, and—this sounds weird—there’s still a small monthly salary piece.

Advisor: Great point—these are gross amounts before taxes. We’ll translate to net later. Now, expenses—what do you two spend per month, all-in, living expenses?
Client 2: Around three thousand a month.
Client 1: It’s a bit higher—about $3,300 a month total.
Advisor: Okay—$3,300 per month. Does that include things like insurance premiums and property taxes, or is it more just day-to-day?
Client 2: It’s kind of everything we pay regularly. Not like big one-off trips.

Client 1: I think it’s around one-forty a year, give or take.
Client 2: Isn’t it closer to one-thirty?
Client 1: No—because of the pension. With that, it’s about $139,950 gross for the year.
Advisor: Got it—about $139,950 gross household. Thanks for correcting that. Now, William, can you break down what makes up your income streams? Even just the buckets.
Client 1: Sure. There’s a pension monthly, Social Security monthly, a little business income monthly, and—this sounds weird—there’s still a small monthly salary piece.

Client 1: I want to say around nine hundred thousand.
Client 2: I thought it was like eight-fifty?
Client 1: Hang on—let me think. It’s about $889,950 total investable assets.
Advisor: One sec—let me note that. $889,950 investable. Now I’m going to ask about each account so nothing gets missed. Emily, do you have a taxable account held at an insurance company? I’m thinking like an annuity or insurance platform account.
Client 2: Yes, that’s mine. It’s taxable, through an insurance company. Value is about $358,850.

Client 1: That’s our buffer, basically.
Advisor: Great. And then the home—primary residence—what do you estimate the value at?
Client 1: About $263,500.
Client 2: Wait, is that what we owe or what it’s worth?
Advisor: Good catch—this is what it’s worth, the home value. And you said earlier no mortgage, so owed would be zero. Does that match?

Client 1: Sure.
Advisor: First one’s simple: what state do you two consider your primary residence—where you’re actually based most of the year?
Client 2: Florida. We’re in Florida.
Client 1: Yep, FL.
Advisor: Got it—Florida. And just to confirm household size: is it just the two of you living in the household—two adults?

Advisor: Hi William, hi Emily—good to meet you both. I’m Alex. Before we dive in, how are you two doing today?
Client 1: Doing alright, thanks. A little tired of paperwork, but we’re fine.
Client 2: Yeah, same. We’re okay—just want this to feel… manageable.
Advisor: Totally hear you. We’ll keep it practical. William, Emily—tell me what prompted you to come in now.
Client 1: We’re retired, and I want a clearer plan for income and taxes. Things feel a bit scattered.

Client 2: Right, sorry—she visits a lot. Two adults.
Advisor: Totally fair, that comes up all the time. So two adults in the household.
Advisor: Next one is kind of industry jargon, so stop me if it sounds weird. Are you working with me through a U.S. registered investment advisor setup—like an RIA—versus, say, a bank branch or an insurance-only agent?
Client 2: Wait, is that like… the market? Like the stock market?
Advisor: Good catch—no, not the stock market. It’s more the channel: US RIA just means the advisory firm model here in the U.S.

Client 1: Yes, that’s what this is. US RIA.
Client 2: Okay—then yes, US RIA.
Advisor: Perfect. And relationship status—just confirming for planning assumptions—are you two married?
Client 2: Yes, married.
Client 1: Married, yes.

Advisor: Busy but good—yeah, I hear you, William. Okay, going back to what you said earlier about wanting this to feel predictable, I want to sanity-check a couple household assumptions that drive the plan.
Client 1: Sure, go ahead.
Advisor: First one: risk comfort. When markets wobble, are you two more conservative, moderate, or aggressive—like, what label fits you best?
Client 2: I’m conservative. I don’t like big swings.
Client 1: I’d say moderate. Not aggressive, but not all cash either.

Client 1: I think it’s around one-forty a year, give or take.
Client 2: Isn’t it closer to one-thirty?
Client 1: No—because of the pension. With that, it’s about $139,950 gross for the year.
Advisor: Got it—about $139,950 gross household. Thanks for correcting that. Now, William, can you break down what makes up your income streams? Even just the buckets.
Client 1: Sure. There’s a pension monthly, Social Security monthly, a little business income monthly, and—this sounds weird—there’s still a small monthly salary piece.

Advisor: Quick tax question, and I know this can get fuzzy. When we talked earlier about taxes, do you know roughly what bracket you land in these days?
Client 2: Is that like… what we owe each year? Because it feels like a lot.
Advisor: Good question—no, not the total you owe. Bracket is the percentage band your last dollars are taxed at. Like 22%, 24%, 32%, 35%—that kind of thing.
Client 1: Right. We’re in the thirty-five percent bracket.
Client 2: I thought it was thirty-two—

Advisor: Hi William, hi Emily—good to meet you both. I’m Alex. Before we dive in, how are you two doing today?
Client 1: Doing alright, thanks. A little tired of paperwork, but we’re fine.
Client 2: Yeah, same. We’re okay—just want this to feel… manageable.
Advisor: Totally hear you. We’ll keep it practical. William, Emily—tell me what prompted you to come in now.
Client 1: We’re retired, and I want a clearer plan for income and taxes. Things feel a bit scattered.

Client 1: I thought it was around ninety-five thousand a year.
Client 2: I thought it was closer to eighty-something, not ninety-five.
Advisor: Okay—so we’ve got a little spread there. William, can we sanity-check it: are we using about $89,850 a year gross for you, or is that off?
Client 1: You’re right—$89,850 gross. My “ninety-five” was me rounding up too aggressively.
Client 2: Thank you. That sounds more like what we’ve been seeing.

Client 2: Yes, joint cash. About $81,550.
Client 1: That’s our buffer, basically.
Advisor: Great. And then the home—primary residence—what do you estimate the value at?
Client 1: About $263,500.
Client 2: Wait, is that what we owe or what it’s worth?

Client 2: Yeah, same. We’re okay—just want this to feel… manageable.
Advisor: Totally hear you. We’ll keep it practical. William, Emily—tell me what prompted you to come in now.
Client 1: We’re retired, and I want a clearer plan for income and taxes. Things feel a bit scattered.
Client 2: And I want to know what it means month to month, honestly.
Advisor: Great. Let’s start with the basics and I’ll stitch it together. First—just confirming household: it’s the two of you, two adults, right?

Client 2: Yeah, same. We’re okay—just want this to feel… manageable.
Advisor: Totally hear you. We’ll keep it practical. William, Emily—tell me what prompted you to come in now.

Client 2: And I want to know what it means month to month, honestly.
Advisor: Great. Let’s start with the basics and I’ll stitch it together. First—just confirming household: it’s the two of you, two adults, right?

Client 1: Right—William is Client 1, Emily is Client 2.
Advisor: Great, thank you. Now, William, I want to confirm one personal detail for planning—your date of birth. What’s your birthday?
Client 1: August 1st, 1957.
Advisor: August 1, 1957—got it.
Client 2: Wait, isn’t it the second? I always mix it up.

Client 2: I think it’s $50,100 gross for me.
Advisor: Perfect—$50,100 gross annually for you, Emily. Let me jot that down.
